name: [TEST FIXTURE] Medications & Triage
sourceAttribution: AUTO-GENERATED TEST FIXTURE — placeholder content, not for production. Real authoring will replace this with cited content.

guides.title: OTC medication categories
guides.content:
[TEST FIXTURE — placeholder. Follow package directions; this is reference only.]

Analgesics: acetaminophen (Tylenol) — pain and fever, easy on the stomach. Ibuprofen (Advil, Motrin) — pain, fever, inflammation, harder on the stomach. Aspirin — pain, fever, inflammation, never for children under 18.

Antihistamines: diphenhydramine (Benadryl) for allergies and sleep; non-drowsy options like loratadine for daytime.

Antacids: calcium carbonate, famotidine, omeprazole for heartburn.

Antidiarrheals: loperamide for adults; not for bacterial diarrhea or children without a doctor.

guides.title: Prescriptions you do not stop abruptly
guides.content:
[TEST FIXTURE — placeholder.]

Some medications cause dangerous withdrawal or rebound effects if stopped suddenly. Discuss any change with the prescriber. Examples include:

Beta blockers (heart, blood pressure) — rebound tachycardia and risk of cardiac event.
Antidepressants, especially SSRIs and SNRIs — discontinuation syndrome.
Benzodiazepines — seizure risk.
Corticosteroids — adrenal insufficiency.
Thyroid replacement — long-term metabolic effects.
Anticoagulants — clotting risk.

Keep at least a 30-day supply on hand. Refill before zero.
guides.sourceRef: [TEST FIXTURE] AUTO-GENERATED — no real source. Real authoring will replace this with a CDC/FEMA/NIH/etc citation.

guides.title: Dangerous interactions
guides.content:
[TEST FIXTURE — placeholder.]

Acetaminophen + alcohol — liver damage.
Warfarin + NSAIDs (ibuprofen, aspirin) — bleeding risk.
MAOIs + many things (other antidepressants, decongestants, certain foods) — hypertensive crisis.
Grapefruit + many medications — alters drug metabolism.
Stimulant + stimulant (cold medicine + ADHD med + caffeine) — cardiac risk.

Keep an updated list of every medication and supplement, including doses. Show it to every provider you see.

guides.title: Mass-casualty triage — START method
guides.content:
[TEST FIXTURE — placeholder.]

START (Simple Triage And Rapid Treatment) sorts casualties into four colors when professional care is overwhelmed:

Green — walking wounded. Move to a holding area.
Yellow — alive, not walking, but breathing on their own and following commands. Delayed care.
Red — unable to walk, breathing only after airway repositioning, OR fast pulse and altered mental status. Immediate care.
Black — not breathing even after airway clear. Care moves to others first.

Reassess regularly — conditions change.
guides.sourceRef: [TEST FIXTURE] AUTO-GENERATED — no real source. Real authoring will replace this with a CDC/FEMA/NIH/etc citation.

guides.title: Wound infection signs
guides.content:
[TEST FIXTURE — placeholder.]

Signs to act on: increasing redness around the wound (especially streaks moving up a limb), increasing pain after the first 48 hours, pus, warmth, swelling, fever, swollen lymph nodes nearby, foul smell.

Clean wounds with clean water (boiled and cooled if possible). Cover with a clean dressing. Get medical care for deep wounds, animal bites, puncture wounds, wounds caused by dirty objects, or wounds in someone whose tetanus booster is more than 5 years old.
